title: What is the FAFSA and Why Do I Have to Fill It Out?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You've heard the acronym. Your parents dread the paperwork. You're not sure why it matters. The FAFSA is the Free Application for Federal Student Aid. It's your gateway to grants, loans, and work-study. Even if you think your family won't qualify, skipping it can cost you thousands. Here is what you need to know.

What the FAFSA Actually Does

The FAFSA collects financial information about your family. It uses that information to calculate your Expected Family Contribution (EFC) — now called the Student Aid Index (SAI). This number is what the government thinks your family can afford to pay for college.

Your SAI determines your eligibility for:
• Federal Pell Grants (free money, no repayment)
• Federal student loans (subsidized and unsubsidized)
• Work-study (part-time jobs funded by the government)
• State grants (many states require FAFSA for state aid)
• Institutional aid (colleges use FAFSA to award their own money)

Even if you don't qualify for need-based aid, you may still qualify for unsubsidized loans. Those are available to almost everyone.

The Biggest Mistake: Skipping It

Many families skip the FAFSA because they think they earn too much for aid. This is a costly mistake. Many colleges require the FAFSA for merit scholarships, not just need-based aid. Skip the FAFSA, and you might be disqualified from scholarships you would have won.

Some private colleges use the CSS Profile for their own aid. But most still require the FAFSA as well. Do both. Don't assume you won't qualify.

When to File

The FAFSA opens on October 1 for the following academic year. File as early as possible. Some aid is first-come, first-served. State grants have deadlines. Don't wait until spring.

You'll need your family's tax information from two years prior. The FAFSA uses 'prior-prior year' taxes. For the 2026-27 school year, you'll use 2024 tax returns.

You can estimate your taxes if you haven't filed yet. You can update the FAFSA later. File early with estimates. Don't wait for taxes to be done.

What You'll Need

Before you start, gather:
• Your Social Security number
• Your parents' Social Security numbers (if dependent)
• Your driver's license number (if you have one)
• Your Alien Registration number (if not a US citizen)
• Federal tax returns (yours and your parents')
• W-2 forms and records of untaxed income
• Bank statements and investment records

You can use the IRS Data Retrieval Tool to automatically import tax information. This is faster and more accurate than typing it manually.

Dependency Status

Most traditional college students are dependents for FAFSA purposes. This means you must provide your parents' financial information, even if they don't pay for your college. FAFSA has strict rules about who is independent. Living on your own doesn't count. Paying your own bills doesn't count.

You are independent if:
• You are 24 or older
• You are married
• You have children who receive more than half their support from you
• You are a veteran or on active duty
• You are a graduate student
• You were in foster care or a ward of the court

If you think your situation qualifies as special circumstances, talk to your college's financial aid office. They can help.

After You File

You'll receive a Student Aid Report (SAR) summarizing your information. Review it for errors. If corrections are needed, make them.

Your SAR will be sent to the colleges you listed on the FAFSA. Each college will use your information to create a financial aid offer. Compare offers carefully. The same family can get different packages from different schools.

The FAFSA is not fun. It is paperwork. But it unlocks money you won't get any other way. Fill it out. File early. Use the money for college.
